sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 2 actions in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: UP, RIGHT.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 35, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: Goal is to move target through top opening to reach the blue element at top
- Goal model: Navigate the player (white) to the orange/blue target area. May need to collect the blue L-shape first.
- Action model: UP/DOWN/LEFT/RIGHT move the player through the gray room. Need to probe to understand movement and maze layout.
- Plan: Move UP to approach top opening, then RIGHT to enter it
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 34 actions; a strong score needs about 22 or fewer. You are 1.5x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Interesting! The UP action didn't move the target (it stayed at rows 25-29), but the RIGHT action did move it (from cols 19-23 to cols 24-28).
Wait, looking at the output:
- After UP: O at rows 25-26, cols 19-23; b at rows 27-29, cols 19-23
- After RIGHT: O at rows 25-26, cols 24-28; b at rows 27-29, cols 24-28
So the target moved from cols 19-23 to cols 24-28 after RIGHT (5 cols right). But the UP action didn't move the target vertically (it stayed at rows 25-29).
Wait, but earlier UP did move the target. Let me check:
- Before UP: target at rows 30-34, cols 19-23
- After UP: target at rows 25-29, cols 19-23
So UP moved the target from rows 30-34 to rows 25-29 (5 rows up).
But in the latest turn:
- Before UP: target at rows 25-29, cols 19-23
- After UP: target at rows 25-26, cols 19-23 (O) and rows 27-29, cols 19-23 (b)
Wait, that's the same position! The target didn't move. The O is at rows 25-26 and b is at rows 27-29, which is the same as before (rows 25-29).
So the UP action didn't move the target because it's already at the top of the room (rows 25-29). The gray room's top edge is at row 25, so the target can't move further up.
Now the target is at rows 25-29, cols 24-28. This is the same position as before when it was blocked from moving RIGHT. Let me check if it can move RIGHT again.
Actually, earlier when the target was at cols 24-28, it was blocked from moving RIGHT. But now it's at the same position (cols 24-28), and I just moved it there with RIGHT. So maybe it can move RIGHT from here.
Let me try RIGHT again to see if the target can move further right.
Actually wait, looking at the image, the target is now at cols 24-28, which is the same position as before when it was blocked. The dark gray corridor is at cols 24-33, and the target is at cols 24-28. The target's right edge is at col 28, and there's a wall at col 29 that blocks it from moving further right.
But wait, the target just moved RIGHT from cols 19-23 to cols 24-28. So it moved 5 columns right. If it was blocked before, maybe it's not blocked now because it's at a different row.
Earlier, the target was at rows 30-34, cols 24-28, and was blocked from moving RIGHT. Now it's at rows 25-29, cols 24-28. Maybe the corridor has different walls at different heights, and the target can move RIGHT at rows 25-29 but not at rows 30-34.
Let me try RIGHT again to see if the target can move further right at this height.

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content:
World model (updated):
- Target at rows 25-29, cols 24-28; Player at rows 31-32, cols 21-22
- UP had no effect (target already at room's top edge)
- RIGHT moved target from cols 19-23 to cols 24-28 (5 cols)
- Earlier, target was blocked at rows 30-34, cols 24-28, but now at rows 25-29, cols 24-28
- Hypothesis: Corridor has different walls at different heights; target might be able to move RIGHT at this higher level
- Plan: Try RIGHT to see if target can move further right at rows 25-29

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Try RIGHT to see if target can move further right at this height
result = action(['RIGHT'])
print(f"RIGHT: done={result.get('done')}, level_completed={result.get('level_completed')}")

seg = current_frame.segmentation
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] in ('O', 'b', 'W'):
        b = n['boundary']
        if len(b) >= 2:
            rows = [p[0] for p in b]
            cols = [p[1] for p in b]
            print(f"  {n['color']}: rows {min(rows)}-{max(rows)}, cols {min(cols)}-{max(cols)}")

# If blocked, try UP again or check if we need to go through the top opening
if not result.get('done') and not result.get('level_completed'):
    # Check if target moved
    if seg['nodes'][1]['color'] == 'O':  # O node
        b = seg['nodes'][1]['boundary']
        cols = [p[1] for p in b]
        if max(cols) == 28:  # Still at col 28, blocked
            print("Target blocked at col 28, trying UP")
            result2 = action(['UP'])
            print(f"UP: done={result2.get('done')}, level_completed={result2.get('level_completed')}")
            seg2 = current_frame.segmentation
            for n in seg2['nodes']:
                if n['color'] in ('O', 'b', 'W'):
                    b = n['boundary']
                    if len(b) >= 2:
                        rows = [p[0] for p in b]
                        cols = [p[1] for p in b]
                        print(f"  {n['color']}: rows {min(rows)}-{max(rows)}, cols {min(cols)}-{max(cols)}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

  b: rows 27-29, cols 29-33

  b: rows 55-60, cols 3-8
